steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a sentence boundaries question. The blank sits between two independent clauses (“Many works … are ______” and “his Geographica … is an extant work”) linked by the conjunctive adverb “conversely.” A conjunctive adverb cannot join independent clauses with a comma alone — it requires a period or semicolon before it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘lost. Conversely,’: Uses a period to end the first independent clause, then begins the second with “Conversely,” correctly punctuated as an introductory word. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘lost and conversely,’: Uses the coordinating conjunction “and” alongside “conversely,” creating an illogical combination. “Conversely” signals contrast, which contradicts the additive meaning of “and.” ✗
  • Choice C — ‘lost, conversely,’: Creates a comma splice. A comma before a conjunctive adverb like “conversely” cannot separate two independent clauses; a semicolon or period is required. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘lost, and conversely’: Uses a comma with the coordinating conjunction “and,” but “and conversely” is grammatically awkward and contradictory in meaning. Additionally, the missing comma after “conversely” leaves the transition word improperly set off. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
